notes: Aberdeen research finding: 95% of Fortune 1000 use EDI

metric_value: 2% of businesses with 5+ employees
notes: Stark adoption gap creating mid-market opportunity for EC Company

metric_name: Traditional EDI VAN transaction fee range
metric_value: $1.00 to $5.00 per transaction
notes: Incumbent pricing that EC Company sought to undercut
Dramatically below-market pricing; EC Start distributed free

metric_name: EC Network monthly service fee (25 tx)
metric_value: $30/month including first 25 transactions at $0.45 each additional
notes: Approaching postage-stamp cost at volume; revolutionary for mid-market EDI

metric_value: $11.1 million through three rounds
notes: Investors: Arthur Andersen audit partnership, Thompson Financial, Huntington Bank

metric_value: Indirect channel via VAR and OEM partners (Arthur Andersen, Huntington Bank, Thompson Financial, Platinum Software, PeopleSoft)
notes: Revenue-sharing model with channel partners creates aligned incentives

metric_value: Founded 1994 as Buena Vista Software, Inc.; rebranded to The EC Company
notes: Three rounds of private equity funding

metric_value: Well-engineered for ease of use; 16-bit; lacks automated scheduling; not yet enterprise messaging server-class
notes: Product described as showing 'youthful immaturity in functionality'

metric_value: Private extranet on outsourced UUNET infrastructure; hub-centric translation; 270 US points of presence
notes: Architect avoids capital expense of own physical network

metric_value: Public Key/Private Key session encryption; symmetric session keys discarded after use
notes: Sound cryptographic model for 1997; consistent with SSL/TLS that later became standard

metric_value: Company estimates 18-24 months to profitable equilibrium on transaction volume model
notes: Aberdeen flags this as ambitious; questions whether low-margin transaction model sustains without professional services

metric_value: May break the mold of traditional EDI VAN suppliers if transaction volume achieved
notes: Conditional endorsement — dependent on channel execution and volume ramp

metric_value: Web-forms via Internet extended from EDI server; Internet-enabled EDI server software at partner sites
notes: GEIS, Sterling, Premenos, Harbinger all targeting mid-market but with legacy VAN architecture

metric_value: First venture investment by Arthur Andersen audit partnership in firm history
notes: Unusual investor; Andersen Consulting (separate entity) not involved

metric_value: The EC Company did not achieve market breakthrough; likely dissolved or quietly acquired during EDI market consolidation 1998-2002
notes: Internet-native EDI ultimately arrived through AS2 (IETF RFC 4130, 2005) not through EC Company; Sterling Commerce and GXS dominated consolidation

metric_value: RFC 4130 AS2 standard adopted 2005; fulfilled EC Company's internet-native EDI vision
notes: Large retailers including Walmart mandated AS2; fulfilled the architectural vision Aberdeen identified in EC Company

metric_value: Sterling Commerce acquired by AT&T (2000), then Oracle (2011) for $1.4B; became Oracle B2B Commerce
notes: Incumbent VAN supplier won consolidation race; EC Company's disruption scenario did not materialize

metric_value: Arthur Andersen dissolved 2002 following Enron accounting scandal
notes: One of EC Company's key channel partners/investors ceased to exist

metric_value: EDI requires EDI standards knowledge, database mapping, per-partner translation — too complex for mid-market IT staff
notes: EC Company's hub-based translation model directly addressed this barrier

metric_value: $1-$5 per transaction creates ROI barrier for low-volume mid-market companies
notes: Aberdeen confirms ROI not compelling for mid-market at incumbent pricing

metric_value: High initial EDI software/hardware investment deters mid-market adoption
notes: EC Company's $130 license addressed this barrier
